ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers lettuce and salad plants broadly; no dedicated red-leaf lettuce sub-code exists in the public FoodEx2 catalogue. 'closest_parent' is used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept specifically for raw red lettuce identified in publicly available SNOMED CT browser; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of raw red lettuce is approximately 47 g (about 1 cup shredded), commonly used as a salad base or sandwich garnish.

nutrient_source.title: Lettuce, red leaf, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169247)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry 'Lettuce, red leaf, raw' is an exact match for raw red lettuce. HPB Singapore values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, and iron are preserved from the input record and are consistent with USDA SR Legacy values within normal analytical variation. USDA SR Legacy is used as the primary approved source for additional micronutrient fields not reported by HPB.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Vegetables — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate for non-starchy leafy greens
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database does not list a direct GI value for raw red lettuce. A GI of approximately 15 is estimated by analogy with other non-starchy leafy greens (e.g., green lettuce, spinach) documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 1.06) / 100 ≈ 0.16, rounded to 0.2. This is consistent with the negligible glycemic impact expected of raw leafy vegetables.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (25 mg/100 g) and meaningful potassium (187 mg/100 g) yield a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio supportive of blood pressure control.
hypertension.guidance: Raw red lettuce is freely recommended for hypertensive individuals under DASH diet principles; its low sodium and moderate potassium content actively support blood pressure management without restriction.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (2.26 g/100 g), negligible sugar (0.48 g/100 g), and estimated GI of 15 result in a glycemic load of approximately 0.2 per serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw red lettuce is an ideal non-starchy vegetable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; its negligible glycemic load and high water content make it suitable for unrestricted inclusion in carbohydrate-controlled meal plans per ADA dietary guidelines.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.03 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and the presence of anthocyanins and folate (36 mcg DFE/100 g) support cardiovascular health.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw red lettuce is appropriate for individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease; its anthocyanin pigments provide antioxidant activity, and its very low saturated fat and cholesterol content align with AHA dietary recommendations.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anthocyanins and vitamin C provide antioxidant activity; calcium (33 mg/100 g) contributes to bone protection relevant in RA.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Raw red lettuce may be included freely in the diet of individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as a low-calorie source of antioxidants and calcium, though it does not independently provide sufficient omega-3 fatty acids or other primary anti-inflammatory nutrients to serve as a therapeutic food for RA management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Raw red lettuce (Lactuca sativa var. crispa, red-leaf type) is a fresh, uncooked leafy vegetable characterised by its deep burgundy-to-green leaves with a mildly bitter, slightly sweet flavour and a crisp, tender texture. It is consumed without cooking, typically as a salad base, sandwich or burger garnish, lettuce wrap filling, or side component in mixed green salads. The red colouration is attributable to anthocyanin pigments concentrated in the leaf margins, which confer antioxidant properties beyond those of green-leaf varieties. Per 100 g, raw red lettuce provides only 13 kcal, 1.33 g protein, 0.22 g fat, and 2.26 g carbohydrate, making it one of the lowest-calorie whole foods available. Sodium is very low at 25 mg, while potassium reaches approximately 187 mg, supporting a favourable electrolyte profile for blood pressure management. Dietary fibre is 0.9 g per 100 g. Micronutrient highlights include vitamin A (175 mcg RAE), folate (36 mcg DFE), vitamin C (3.7 mg), calcium (33 mg), and iron (1.2 mg). Its estimated glycemic index of approximately 15 and glycemic load of 0.2 per standard serving make it clinically suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. Raw red lettuce is vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free, halal, and kosher, and is widely used across global cuisines as a fresh salad ingredient.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced from HPB Singapore (input, confidence 0.95) and corroborated by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169247), an exact match for raw red leaf lettuce. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, clearly documented. All values are internally consistent and plausible for a raw leafy vegetable.
notes: 1. Core macros (protein_g 1.33, fat_g 0.22, carbohydrate_g 2.26) are all non-zero and sourced from HPB Singapore; no zero-macro justification is required. 2. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 (confirmed negligible/zero per USDA SR Legacy for raw lettuce). cholesterol_mg is set to 0.0 (confirmed zero for plant food). vitamin_b12_mcg is set to 0.0 (naturally zero in plant foods). vitamin_d_mcg is set to 0.0 (naturally zero in raw lettuce per USDA SR Legacy). 3. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.01), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.11), saturated_fat_g (0.03), potassium_mg (187.0), vitamin_c_mg (3.7), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (175.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.06), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.08), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (0.32), vitamin_b6_mg (0.1), folate_mcg_dfe (36.0), vitamin_e_mg (0.44), phosphorus_mg (28.0), magnesium_mg (12.0), zinc_mg (0.2), selenium_mcg (0.6), water_g (95.6) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 169247 (exact match). 4. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for non-starchy leafy greens; no direct GI measurement for raw red lettuce was found in the database as of 2026-04-16. 5. Rheumatoid arthritis suitability from input has been reclassified to suitable=true with low concern level, as the input record contained an internal contradiction (reason stated anti-inflammatory benefits but suitable was set to false). The revised assessment reflects that red lettuce is a beneficial food for RA patients as a low-calorie antioxidant source, while noting it is not a primary therapeutic anti-inflammatory food. 6. FoodEx2 code A00LC is a broader category match; no dedicated red-leaf lettuce sub-code exists in the public FoodEx2 catalogue. The foodex2_match_type has been corrected to 'closest_parent' as per validator feedback.
